purpose: Defines the `!add_task [--dashboard {id}] {prompt}` command for injecting new tasks into an active swarm mid-flight. The master must validate the swarm is active (initialization.json non-null), read existing state (initialization.json + all progress files + master_state.json + plan.json), perform deep analysis of the new prompt, decompose into 1+ subtasks, resolve forward and reverse dependencies (including bidirectional rewiring of pending tasks' depends_on), assign waves and IDs ({wave}.{next_index}), update initialization.json and plan.json atomically, append log entries, update master_state.json, and run an eager dispatch round. Produces additional agents[]/tasks[] entries plus dispatch logs.
gotchas: NEVER modify depends_on for completed or in-progress tasks — only pending tasks can have their dependencies rewired in Step 7B
File conflicts with in-progress tasks must trigger a user warning and confirmation prompt — they are not auto-rejected
Topological sort across the FULL graph (existing + new) is required to detect cycles before writing
The `ide` dashboard is reserved for the IDE agent and never valid for !add_task
If no active swarm exists (task is null or agents[] empty), the command MUST stop and direct the user to !p_track
Reverse dependency rewiring (Step 7B) is the most error-prone step — pending tasks must be updated in BOTH initialization.json and plan.json
patterns: 13-step procedure: parse args → read swarm state → read plan → read project context → deep analysis → decompose → resolve deps in 4 sub-steps (forward, reverse, inter-task, validation) → assign waves/IDs → update initialization.json → update plan.json → log → update master_state.json → eager dispatch
Dependency resolution is bidirectional — new tasks may depend on existing ones AND existing pending tasks may be rewired to depend on new ones
Wave assignment uses dependency depth: completed deps → lowest sensible wave; pending/in-progress deps → max(dep wave) + 1; new wave creation when wave > total_waves
Task IDs follow {wave}.{next_index} format, computed by scanning highest existing index in target wave
Atomic write protocol: read → parse → modify in memory → stringify → write the full file
conventions: Top-level command header with backticked syntax line, Purpose, Syntax, Prerequisites, Steps (numbered), Edge Cases, and 'What This Command Does NOT Do' sections
Tabular field definitions for task schema (title/description/context/critical/files/tags/layer/directory)
Inline JSON examples with placeholder syntax {field_name}
Bold callouts ('Atomic write', 'NEVER modify') for non-negotiable rules
